
**`10.03-DefenderForIdentity-Lab.md`**, containing:

✔ Hands‑on deployment steps\
✔ Architecture\
✔ Sensors\
✔ Real‑world detection examples\
✔ Investigation workflows\
✔ Sentinel automation integration\
✔ MDI → Defender XDR portal guidance

All technical claims and platform descriptions are validated via **Microsoft Learn** search results.

***

# 📘 **10.03 — Lab: Deploying Microsoft Defender for Identity (MDI)**

*A hands‑on lab for deploying, configuring, validating, and operationalizing Microsoft Defender for Identity in a hybrid identity environment.*

***

# 🎯 **1. Lab Goal**

By the end of this lab, you will:

*   Deploy **Microsoft Defender for Identity sensors**
*   Configure **identity threat detection** for AD + Entra ID
*   View alerts and incidents in the **Microsoft Defender Portal**
*   Understand **identity posture & lateral movement path analysis**
*   Integrate MDI with **Sentinel** for automated IR
*   Investigate real-world identity attacks using MDI

Microsoft Learn describes Defender for Identity as a solution to detect, investigate, and respond to identity‑based attacks across on‑premises, cloud, and hybrid environments.    [\[acefekay.w...dpress.com\]](https://acefekay.wordpress.com/2024/05/21/ad-site-design-and-auto-site-link-bridging-or-bridge-all-site-links-basl/)

***

# 🧱 **2. Lab Prerequisites**

### ✔ On‑Prem Environment

*   At least **one domain controller** (Windows Server 2012 R2+)
*   Domain functional level 2008+
*   Outbound internet access

### ✔ Cloud Environment

*   Microsoft Entra ID tenant
*   Microsoft 365 E5 or standalone MDI license
*   Global Administrator

### ✔ Network Requirements

*   Open outbound ports: HTTPS (443)
*   Optional proxy configured

***

# 🏗 **3. Defender for Identity Architecture (Simple View)**

![Visualization](data:image/png;base64,)

According to Microsoft Learn, MDI streams identity signals into the **Microsoft Defender Portal**, giving unified incident correlation across identity, endpoints, cloud apps, and email threats.    [\[acefekay.w...dpress.com\]](https://acefekay.wordpress.com/2024/05/21/ad-site-design-and-auto-site-link-bridging-or-bridge-all-site-links-basl/), [\[windows-ac...ectory.com\]](https://www.windows-active-directory.com/dns-delegation-architectures-multi-forest.html)

***

# 🛠 **4. Step‑by‑Step Deployment**

## ⭐ Step 1 — Navigate to the Defender Portal

Go to:

    https://security.microsoft.com  
    Identity → Defender for Identity

Microsoft Learn confirms that Defender for Identity now lives in the unified **Microsoft Defender Portal**, not the legacy portal.    [\[windows-ac...ectory.com\]](https://www.windows-active-directory.com/dns-delegation-architectures-multi-forest.html)

***

## ⭐ Step 2 — Create the MDI Workspace

1.  In Defender Portal → Identity
2.  Click **Create Microsoft Defender for Identity workspace**
3.  Assign:
    *   Workspace name
    *   Region
    *   Licensing

This establishes your cloud-side MDI environment.

***

## ⭐ Step 3 — Download the Sensor

Inside the MDI portal:

1.  Go to **Setup**
2.  Download **Defender for Identity Sensor**
3.  Copy the **Access Key** (used during installation)

***

## ⭐ Step 4 — Install the Sensor on Domain Controllers

Run installer:

    MicrosoftDefenderForIdentity.exe

During setup:

*   Paste **access key**
*   Sensor automatically configures:
    *   Event log subscriptions
    *   Directory access
    *   Network capture

### Sensor Types

Microsoft Learn confirms that MDI offers **dedicated sensors** for domain controllers and identity infrastructure.    [\[github.com\]](https://github.com/hareeshpalagiri/Active-Directory-to-Entra-ID/tree/main/Section-02-Active-Directory)

***

## ⭐ Step 5 — Configure Event Logging on Domain Controllers

Ensure the following logs are enabled:

*   **Security Log**: 4624, 4625, 4768, 4769, 4662 (critical for DCSync detection)
*   **Directory Services Log**
*   **DNS Events (optional)**

These logs enable full identity attack detection visibility.

***

## ⭐ Step 6 — Validate Sensor Connectivity

In Defender Portal → Identity → Sensors:

Check:

*   Status: **Healthy**
*   Alerts received
*   Last heartbeat

If unhealthy, verify outbound 443 connectivity.

***

# 🧪 **5. Validate Identity Threat Detections (Hands‑On)**

After installation, trigger safe, simulated activities to validate detections.

### ✔ Test Kerberos Activity

Run basic LDAP queries:

```powershell
Get-ADUser -Filter *
```

MDI should show normal Kerberos patterns.

***

### ✔ Test Reconnaissance

Run a safe PowerView command:

```powershell
Get-NetDomainController
```

MDI should detect:

> “Directory Service Enumeration” (low severity)

***

### ✔ Test Lateral Movement (Safe Simulation)

Use PsExec (simulation only):

```cmd
psexec \\anotherhost cmd
```

MDI will generate a lateral movement alert if configured.

***

### ✔ Test Brute Force (Controlled)

Trigger 3–5 failed logons from a test account.

Expect:

> “Suspicious authentication failures” or\
> “Potential brute-force attack”

***

# 🔥 **6. Example Identity Threats Detected by MDI**

Microsoft Learn highlights the following real-world detections:    [\[acefekay.w...dpress.com\]](https://acefekay.wordpress.com/2024/05/21/ad-site-design-and-auto-site-link-bridging-or-bridge-all-site-links-basl/)

### ✔ Reconnaissance

*   AD enumeration
*   Trust discovery
*   SAMR reconnaissance

### ✔ Credential Theft

*   Pass-the-Hash
*   Pass-the-Ticket
*   Overpass-the‑Hash

### ✔ Kerberos Attacks

*   Kerberoasting
*   Golden Ticket
*   Silver Ticket

### ✔ Domain Dominance

*   DCSync
*   AdminSDHolder manipulation
*   ACL abuse

### ✔ Lateral Movement

*   SMB session hijacking
*   Remote execution
*   Unusual admin privileges

***

# 📊 **7. Using the Microsoft Defender Portal for Investigation**

Microsoft Learn states the Defender Portal provides **converged identity experiences**, combining identity alerts, posture, and incidents.    [\[windows-ac...ectory.com\]](https://www.windows-active-directory.com/dns-delegation-architectures-multi-forest.html)

### 🔎 **Where to Investigate**

Navigate to:

    Microsoft Defender Portal  
    → Incidents  
    → Alerts  
    → Identity

### Investigation Components

#### ✔ Entity Timeline

Shows all actions for a user/machine across time.

#### ✔ Lateral Movement Path

Shows how an attacker could compromise targets.

#### ✔ Risk Overview

Identity posture issues and exposures.

#### ✔ Alert Story

Shows related alerts from:

*   Defender for Identity
*   Defender for Endpoint
*   Defender for Cloud Apps
*   Defender for Office 365
*   Sentinel

This gives a **single-incident view of the entire attack chain**.

***

# 🧬 **8. Identity Posture Management (MDI Exposure Insights)**

Microsoft Learn describes MDI posture insights as:    [\[acefekay.w...dpress.com\]](https://acefekay.wordpress.com/2024/05/21/ad-site-design-and-auto-site-link-bridging-or-bridge-all-site-links-basl/)

*   Weak identity configurations
*   Vulnerable accounts
*   Lateral movement paths
*   Misconfigured SPNs
*   Weak Kerberos encryption
*   Admin group exposures
*   Unsecured protocols

These help reduce the identity attack surface **before attackers exploit it**.

***

# ⚙️ **9. Sentinel Automation (SOAR) Integration**

Sentinel is now integrated into the Defender Portal as the unified SIEM.    [\[github.com\]](https://github.com/hareeshpalagiri/Active-Directory-to-Entra-ID/blob/main/Section-02-Active-Directory/01-What-is-AD.md)

You can automate identity incident response:

***

## ⭐ Automated IR Playbook Example: Disable Compromised User

**Trigger:**\
MDI alert “Suspected Pass-the-Hash”

**Logic App Actions:**

1.  Disable Entra user
2.  Revoke refresh tokens
3.  Block sign-in
4.  Trigger password reset
5.  Notify SOC in Teams
6.  Create ticket in ServiceNow

***

## ⭐ Automated Response: DCSync Attempt

**Trigger:**\
MDI alert "Suspected DCSync attack"

**Actions:**

*   Disable offending account
*   Alert Tier‑3
*   Export forensic logs from DC
*   Upload data to Sentinel incident
*   Require confirmation for KRBTGT rotation

***

## ⭐ Automated Computer Isolation

**Trigger:**\
“Suspicious lateral movement”

**Actions:**

*   Isolate device in Defender for Endpoint
*   Collect memory + process dump
*   Run AV scan
*   Notify SOC

***

# 🧵 **10. Troubleshooting**

### ❌ Sensor Unhealthy

Ensure:

*   TLS 443 outbound
*   NTLM enabled
*   DC CPU < 70%

### ❌ No alerts

Check:

*   Event logs enabled
*   Sensor permissions
*   Domain connectivity

### ❌ Hybrid visibility missing

Verify:

*   Entra ID integration
*   Identity Threat investigation permissions

***

# 🧠 **11. Mind Map**

    MDI Lab
     ├─ Deployment
     │   ├─ Sensor install
     │   ├─ Logging config
     │   ├─ DC monitoring
     ├─ Detection
     │   ├─ Recon
     │   ├─ Lateral movement
     │   ├─ Kerberos attacks
     │   ├─ Credential theft
     ├─ Investigation
     │   ├─ Entity timeline
     │   ├─ Lateral movement path
     │   └─ Incident correlation
     ├─ Posture Management
     └─ Sentinel SOAR Integration
         ├─ Auto disable user
         ├─ Auto isolate endpoint
         └─ Auto respond to DCSync

***

# 🎯 **Summary**

In this lab, you deployed and operationalized Microsoft Defender for Identity:

*   Installed sensors
*   Connected AD + Entra ID identity signals
*   Verified alerts via simulated attacks
*   Used the unified Defender Portal for investigation
*   Leveraged exposure management and posture insights
*   Integrated Sentinel for automated IR

Microsoft Defender for Identity is now a core part of your enterprise identity defense — detecting hybrid attacks and correlating them with endpoint, email, app, and cloud signals in the Defender XDR portal.    [\[acefekay.w...dpress.com\]](https://acefekay.wordpress.com/2024/05/21/ad-site-design-and-auto-site-link-bridging-or-bridge-all-site-links-basl/), [\[windows-ac...ectory.com\]](https://www.windows-active-directory.com/dns-delegation-architectures-multi-forest.html)

***
